cards.text: To make a function virtual, place the {{c1::virtual}} keyword before the function declaration.

cards.back: A special type of member function that, when called, resolves to the most-derived version of the function for the actual type of the object being referenced or pointed to.

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: Because Base::getName() is virtual, calling it through a Base reference resolves to the most-derived version (Derived::getName()).

cards.text: A derived function that has the same signature and return type as a base virtual function is called an {{c1::override}}.

cards.front: When does virtual function resolution work?
cards.back:
Only when a virtual member function is called through a pointer or reference to a class type object.

It does NOT work when called directly on an object (e.g., obj.func() always calls the object's type version).

cards.text: {{c1::Compile-time polymorphism}} includes function overload resolution and template resolution, while {{c2::runtime polymorphism}} includes virtual function resolution.

cards.back: The ability of an entity to have multiple forms (literally "many forms")

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: Virtual function resolution finds the most-derived version between A and C. Since C doesn't override getName(), it resolves to B::getName().

cards.text: If a function is marked as virtual, all matching overrides in derived classes are {{c1::implicitly}} considered virtual, even if not explicitly marked.

cards.front: Why should you never call virtual functions from constructors or destructors?
cards.back:
In constructors: The derived portion hasn't been created yet, so it will call the base version instead.

In destructors: The derived portion has already been destroyed, so it will always call the base version.

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: Derived::getValue() is not considered a matching override because the return type (double) doesn't match Base::getValue()'s return type (int). The signature must match exactly.

cards.concept: The key benefit of virtual functions
cards.explanation:
Virtual functions allow you to structure code so that newly derived classes automatically work with existing code without modification.

You can write a function once using base class references/pointers, and it will work correctly with all current and future derived classes.
examples: // Works with Cat, Dog, and any future Animal types
// No need to modify report() when adding new animals

cards.text: Virtual functions have two downsides: {{c1::resolving a virtual function call takes longer}} than a regular one, and the compiler must allocate an {{c2::extra pointer}} for each object of a class with virtual functions.

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: B::getName() is not const, so it doesn't match the signature of A::getName() const. It's not an override, so the call resolves to A::getName().

For a derived function to be an override, it must EXACTLY match the base function's signature:
  • Same function name
  • Same parameter types
  • Same const-ness
  • Same return type (with rare exceptions for covariant types)
If any part doesn't match, it's not an override and won't resolve virtually.
examples: virtual int func();        // NOT an override (missing const)
virtual double func() const;  // NOT an override (wrong return)
virtual int func(int) const;  // NOT an override (extra param)
